text.title: Competitive Intelligence: Rules and Antipatterns
text.summary: Hard rules for evidence discipline, snapshot diffing, and agent-specific failure modes with concrete mitigations.

rule.statement: Date every competitive report as competitive-analysis-YYYY-MM-DD.md. Store in version control so quarterly re-runs produce diffable deltas. The deltas are the actual decision input, not the absolute values.
rule.rationale: A one-time matrix decays in 3-6 months; without a re-run cadence the analysis becomes a stale anchor that biases decisions long after market conditions changed.

rule.statement: Visualping the top-5 competitor pricing pages weekly. A weekly diff on pricing pages provides 80% of the "competitive monitoring" value for minimal cost.
rule.rationale: Pricing changes are the highest-severity competitive event; daily manual checking is impractical, while a weekly automated diff catches changes within the relevant decision window.

rule.statement: Never act on a whitespace cell found in the current session. Log it, validate against demand signals (review mining or interview transcripts), decide in the next run.
rule.rationale: A 2-week sanity gap prevents impulsive roadmap additions based on a single session's gap analysis that may be incomplete or based on missing evidence.

antipattern.description: Treating the competitive analysis as a standing document rather than a dated snapshot.
antipattern.reason: A feature matrix without a capture date is an undated fact claim that degrades silently. Readers cannot tell if they are looking at last week's reality or last year's.

antipattern.description: Running the feature matrix without synonym merging across competitor feature names.
antipattern.reason: The same capability under different names produces multiple rows with conflicting Y/N values, making aggregation impossible and gap identification unreliable.

antipattern.description: Treating listed prices on enterprise tiers as ACVs for B2B pricing analysis.
antipattern.reason: "Talk to Sales" tiers are anchors, not ACVs — actual discounts can be 50-80% off list. Tag these rows with confidence: low, and cross-reference with G2 reviewer-reported pricing.

antipattern.description: Marketing-page parroting: LLM paraphrases the competitor's homepage tagline as "positioning" without checking third-party sources.
antipattern.reason: Force evidence_urls length ≥2 with at least one third-party source. Homepage taglines are marketing copy, not observable positioning in buyer perception.

antipattern.description: Numeric hallucination: funding totals, ARR, headcount fabricated without source_url.
antipattern.reason: Reject any numeric field without source_url. "Unknown" is the correct output when API access is absent, not a plausible invention.

antipattern.description: Optimistic gap recommendations: LLM default helpful tone recommends every gap as an opportunity without adversarial review.
antipattern.reason: The gap-validator critic with a forced skip budget ≥40% is non-negotiable. Without it, the gap list becomes a feature-bloat backlog.

antipattern.description: Token blowup from passing full G2 review HTML to the matrix filler.
antipattern.reason: Never paste full review HTML into the matrix-filler. The review-miner must distill to top-5 complaints per competitor first, then pass the distillation.

antipattern.description: Human-in-the-loop checkpoint missing: gap list becomes spec input without founder review of the kill-list rationale.
antipattern.reason: Agents under-skip (helpful bias), founders over-skip (loss aversion) — the diff between their lists is the real shortlist. Skip the checkpoint and you get either feature bloat or missed opportunities.
